narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 52% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold we use to flag higher-risk applicants. Looking at your profile, you ranked in the 4th decile for Year 1 grades at your school—below the typical 6th decile—and dropped to the 1st decile for Year 3, which is much lower than the average 6th decile there. Your LSAT score of 30 falls short of the usual 37, your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 is under the standard 3.2, and your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket compared to most applicants' middle-range status. As a male Asian student planning full-time enrollment, you're in line with common demographics, but those didn't sway things much here.

The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 decile ranking, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure due to how poorly it stacks up against peers. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also pushed in the same concerning direction by being notably below average. Your Year 1 decile came next in influence, reinforcing the risk with its subpar standing, followed by your family's income level, which added a modest pull toward the failure side. Your racial background as Asian had a smaller but still negative impact on the prediction, then your undergraduate GPA contributed similarly, with your full-time plans and gender playing very minor roles—all tilting toward higher failure odds rather than success.

In essence, your academic deciles, especially in Year 3, and LSAT stood out as the top drivers, far outweighing other factors like income or demographics when compared to typical students who pass the bar. Students who succeed usually have stronger deciles around the 6th, LSATs near 37, and GPAs above 3.2, putting you at a clear disadvantage in those key areas. To boost your future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 37 or higher, as that's something you can realistically improve with targeted prep. Building better study habits now could help lift your grades in any remaining coursework, mimicking the stronger deciles of successful peers. While family income and background are harder to shift, excelling in these controllable academics has turned around predictions for many in similar spots—keep pushing, and reapply once you've strengthened those.
